Lesson_Notes.Title: Chris and His Dislike for Art
Lesson_Notes.Details: Chris was a little boy who enjoyed school and doing many things, except art and writing. Using brushes and pencils was difficult for him, so his artwork never turned out well. Because of this, he usually gave up quickly and never practiced for more than ten minutes at a time, often doing it angrily.

Lesson_Notes.Title: The Magic Pencil and The Talking Drawing
Lesson_Notes.Details: One day, Chris found a pencil with lovely colours. He tried to draw a circle, but it didn't go well, and he was about to throw the pencil away. Suddenly, the drawing began to speak to him, asking him to draw a pair of eyes so it could see. Chris, although shocked, drew two little spots inside the circle. The circle complained about how badly Chris had drawn it.

Lesson_Notes.Title: The Practice Session
Lesson_Notes.Details: The little drawing told Chris that if he tried again, he would do better. It asked Chris to rub it out and draw another one. Chris erased it and drew another imperfect circle. The drawing with its quick, squeaky voice reminded him about the eyes and said it had to teach him how to draw. Chris spent the whole afternoon drawing and erasing. The drawing made funny remarks like 'Draw me some hair, quickly, I look like a lollipop!'

Lesson_Notes.Title: The Truth Learned
Lesson_Notes.Details: After a whole afternoon of practice, Chris could draw the little figure better than most of his classmates. Before going to bed, Chris thanked his 'new teacher' (the drawing). However, the drawing replied that it didn't do anything. It pointed out that Chris had finally practiced a lot and actually enjoyed doing it, which was the real reason he improved. Chris realized this was true.

Lesson_Notes.Title: The Next Morning
Lesson_Notes.Details: The next morning, Chris couldn't find the magic pencil. The sheet of paper was blank, with only rubbing out marks. He was worried if it had been a dream. To check, he took a normal pencil and tried to draw the little man. It turned out very well, except for a few rough lines. He imagined the little teacher telling him to fix them, so he did.

Lesson_Notes.Title: The Final Lesson
Lesson_Notes.Details: Chris realized that the magic pencil didn't make a difference. To learn to do things, one only needed to keep trying (persevere) and enjoy the process. From that day on, whenever Chris drew or painted, he had fun imagining his drawing telling him to do better.

Lesson_Notes.Title: Let Us Read Aloud (Megala's Race)
Lesson_Notes.Details: The school was decorated for Annual Sports Day. Megala participated in the 800 meters running race against district and divisional winners. During the race (which had two laps), she was in fifth place at the end of the first lap. Suddenly, she fell. Before anyone could help, she got up and continued running. Though she came last, everyone cheered for her. The headmaster gave her a special prize because of her perseverance and not giving up.

Lesson_Notes.Title: Let Us Write (Story Writing Using Picture Boards)
Lesson_Notes.Details:
This section teaches how to write a story using pictures. The example is the classic 'Cap Seller and the Monkeys' story.
Steps:
1. Look at the picture and find the characters.
2. Look at the picture and find what they are doing.
3. Write it in a simple sentence.
4. Repeat the steps.

Book_Back_Questions.Title: A. Choose the best answer
Questions_and_Answers:
1. Chris enjoyed doing all sorts of things except, writing and _____
Ans: b. drawing

2. One day Chris tried to draw a _____
Ans: b. circle

3. Pencil asked him to draw _____
Ans: a. pair of eyes

4. Draw me some hair, quickly, I look like a _____
Ans: b. lollipop

5. Before going to bed that night, Chris gave his new teacher a _____
Ans: a. hearty thanks

Book_Back_Questions.Title: B. Answer the following questions
Questions_and_Answers:
1. What was the truth finally learned by Chris? 
Ans: Chris learned that to learn to do things, you only need to keep trying and to enjoy while doing so.

2. How can we do our work better? 
Ans: We can do our work better by practicing continuously, not giving up, and enjoying the work.

3. Why did he rub the circle again and again? 
Ans: He rubbed the circle again and again because his drawing told him to rub it out and try to draw it better.

4. Why was Chris worried? 
Ans: Chris was worried because the next morning he couldn't find the magic pencil and the paper was completely blank. He didn't know if it was real or a dream.

5. What is the one thing you will try and improve after reading the story? How? 
Ans: (Student's own perspective answer, generally related to practicing something they are weak at).

Book_Back_Questions.Title: Let us read aloud - Answer the following questions
Questions_and_Answers:
1. What was the event Megala participated? 
Ans: Megala participated in the 800 meters running race.

2. Why did everyone support Megala? 
Ans: Everyone supported her because even after she fell on the ground, she immediately got up and started to run again instead of giving up.

3. What is the main idea of the story? 
Ans: The main idea is about perseverance—never giving up, even when things go wrong or you fall down.

4. Why did the headmaster give Megala a special prize? 
Ans: The headmaster gave her a special prize for her spirit, courage, and perseverance in completing the race despite falling.

Book_Back_Questions.Title: I Can Do
Questions_and_Answers:
A. 1. The child got an _____ for winning the race. 
Ans: a. award

A. 2. I got a _____ to see my favourite heroine. 
Ans: b. chance

B. Match with the opposite gender:
1. prince - princess
2. hero - heroine
3. bridegroom - bride

C. Write the correct word (Masculine-Feminine pairs based on pictures/words given): 
rooster - hen
tiger - tigress
king - queen

E. Match the rhyming words:
1. earn - learn
2. fend - end
3. glow - slow
4. play - day

F. Fill in the blanks with present continuous tense of the given word:
a. They are eating their breakfast.
b. He is reading a news paper.
c. Bharathi is writing his home work.

Quiz_Questions.Question: What were the two things Chris did not enjoy doing?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The text states: 'Chris was a little boy who enjoyed going to school and doing all sorts of things except art and writing.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: Why did Chris usually give up quickly on his artwork?
Options: Because using brushes and pencils was not easy for him
Because he was too busy playing
Quiz_Questions.Answer: Because using brushes and pencils was not easy for him
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The story explains, 'Using brushes and pencils was not easy for him. So, his artwork was never a happy ending, and he would give up quickly.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What did Chris find that he could not resist using to draw a circle?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The text says: 'But one day Chris found a pencil of such lovely colours that he could not resist...'

Quiz_Questions.Question: When Chris was about to throw the pencil away, what happened?
Options: His drawing began to speak to him
Quiz_Questions.Answer: His drawing began to speak to him
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The story says, 'Just then, his drawing began to speak to him.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What was the very first thing the drawing asked Chris to draw on it?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The drawing said, 'At least draw me a pair of eyes.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: How did Chris draw the eyes?
Options: He drew two little spots inside the circle
He drew them outside the circle
Quiz_Questions.Answer: He drew two little spots inside the circle
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The text notes: '...he managed to draw two little spots inside the circle.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What was Chris's excuse when the circle complained about looking bad?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: Chris replied, 'I don't draw very well... trying to make excuses.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What kind of voice did the circle have?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The text describes it as: '...said the circle with its quick, squeaky little voice.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What did the drawing say Chris looked like before he drew hair on it?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The drawing shouted: 'Draw me some hair, quickly, I look like a lollipop!'

Quiz_Questions.Question: After practicing for the whole afternoon, how good was Chris at drawing the little figure?
Options: He was still very bad at it
He could draw it better than most of his classmates
He could draw it perfectly like a famous artist
Quiz_Questions.Answer: He could draw it better than most of his classmates
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The text states: 'Chris could now draw the little figure much better than most of his classmates.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: Who did Chris thank before going to bed?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The text says: 'Chris gave his new teacher a hearty thanks for teaching him to draw.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What was the real reason Chris had drawn so badly in the past?
Options: He never practiced more than ten minutes at a time and did it angrily
He only liked painting, not drawing
Quiz_Questions.Answer: He never practiced more than ten minutes at a time and did it angrily
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: Chris stopped to think and realized: 'He had drawn so badly because he had never practised more than ten minutes at a time. He had always done it angrily.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: Where did Chris keep his pencil before going to sleep?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The story says: 'He carefully kept the pencil in his school bag before going to sleep.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What did Chris find the next morning when he looked for his drawing?
Options: The little man was still talking
The sheet of paper was completely blank with only rubbing marks
The drawing had jumped off the page
Quiz_Questions.Answer: The sheet of paper was completely blank with only rubbing marks
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The text states: 'The sheet of paper on which he had drawn the little figure, although still full of rubbing out marks, was completely blank.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: How did Chris 'settle the matter' of whether he was dreaming or not?
Options: He took a normal pencil and paper and tried to draw the little man
Quiz_Questions.Answer: He took a normal pencil and paper and tried to draw the little man
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The text explains: 'So, to settle the matter, he took a pencil and paper and tried to draw a little man.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What did Chris realize about the magic pencil at the end of the story?
Options: He needed it to draw well
It made no difference whether he had the magic pencil or not
It was stolen by his friend
Quiz_Questions.Answer: It made no difference whether he had the magic pencil or not
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: He realized: 'it made no difference whether you had the magic pencil or not.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: According to the story, what two things are needed to learn to do things?
Options: A magic pencil and good paper
To keep trying and to enjoy while doing so
To work fast and be serious
Quiz_Questions.Answer: To keep trying and to enjoy while doing so
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The moral of the story is explicitly stated: 'To learn to do things, you only needed to keep trying and to enjoy while doing so.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: In the 'Let Us Read Aloud' passage, what event did Megala participate in?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The passage says, 'The next event was 800 meters running. Megala was in the race.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: How many laps was the race in the passage?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The text states, 'All had to finish two laps.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What position was Megala in at the end of the first lap?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The passage reads, 'At the end of the first lap, Megala was in the fifth place.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What happened to Megala during the race?
Options: She ran out of the track
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The story says, 'Suddenly, she fell on the ground.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: Even though Megala came last, who gave her a special prize?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The passage concludes: 'She had come last, but the headmaster gave her a special prize.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: What is the very first step in writing a story using a picture board?
Options: Write it in a simple sentence
Look at the picture and find the characters
Quiz_Questions.Answer: Look at the picture and find the characters
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The instruction states: 'Step 1: Look at the picture and find the characters.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: In the 'I Can Do' section, complete the sentence: Bharathi _____ his home work.
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The instruction asks for present continuous tense. Since Bharathi is a singular subject (He), the correct form is 'is writing'.

Quiz_Questions.Question:
Assertion (A): Chris's first circle was a perfect round.
Reason (R): He used a magic pencil with lovely colours.
Options: Both A and R are true
Both A and R are false
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true
Quiz_Questions.Answer: A is false, but R is true
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The assertion is false because 'As always, it did not go well'. The reason is true; he did use a magic pencil. Therefore, A is false, but R is true.

Quiz_Questions.Question: Analogy: Masculine : Feminine :: Prince : ?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The feminine equivalent of prince is princess, as shown in the 'I Can Do' matching section.

Quiz_Questions.Question: Life Situation: If you try riding a bicycle and fall down, but you immediately get up and try again, whose attitude are you reflecting from this lesson?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: Megala fell during her race but immediately got up and finished it, showing perseverance.

Quiz_Questions.Question: According to the cap seller story in the picture board section, what did the monkeys do when he threw his cap down?
Options: They threw his cap back to him
They also threw their caps down
Quiz_Questions.Answer: They also threw their caps down
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: The text says: 'He threw his cap down and the monkeys also did so.'

Quiz_Questions.Question: In the 'I Can Do' section, what rhyming word matches with 'slow'?
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: From the poem 'Never Give Up' and the exercise, 'glow' rhymes with 'slow'.

Quiz_Questions.Question: Identify the incorrect present continuous sentence from the options below based on the 'I Can Do' exercise rules.
Options: He is reading a news paper.
Bharathi are writing his home work.
Quiz_Questions.Answer: Bharathi are writing his home work.
Quiz_Questions.Explanation: Bharathi is singular, so it must be 'is writing', not 'are writing'.
